      251 -     <div style={{ width: '100%', height: '800px', borderRadius: '16px', overflow: 'hidden', border: '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1)', background: 'rgba(0,0,0,0.5)' }}>

      251 +     <div style={{ width: '100%', height: '600px', borderRadius: '16px', overflowY: 'auto', border: '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1)', background: 'rgba(0,0,0,0.5)', padding: '40px', boxSizing:
          'border-box' }}>
      252 +         <div style={{ maxWidth: '800px', margin: '0 auto', color: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.8)', lineHeight: 1.8, fontSize: '14px' }}>
      253 +             <h3 style={{ fontSize: '24px', fontWeight: 900, color: 'white', letterSpacing: '0.1em', marginBottom: '30px', borderBottom: '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1)', paddingBottom: '10px'
          }}>Understanding Ticketing Systems</h3>
      254 +             
      255 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What information should a user provide when creating a
          ticket?</h4>
      256 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>When a user creates a ticket they should describe the experience of the client and their problem needing to be resolved. The documentation should
          include the client's name, details of the issue, whatever is needed to access the machine, and a clear description of what information was gathered during their conversation. They should also
          document whatever decisions were made to try and fix the issue if there were any steps taken.</p>
      257 + 
      258 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why is it important to categorize tickets?</h4>
      259 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>It's important to categorize tickets because we need to resolve tickets in the order of impact or reach and to imply the escalation level if it has
          not already been assigned/claimed by that Support team. It gives clarity on which tickets to prioritize other than the SLA timing. Also it is important for organizational purposes because there can
          be a lot of tickets coming in sometimes depending on the state of the organization. So it's good to have categorization to keep a birds eye view on what sort of tickets are coming in and it can
          reveal a larger, more connected issue than just there being a lot of individual tickets coming in. This gives you the ability to get to the core of an issue that resolves multiple tickets at once
          saving companies a lot of time.</p>
      260 + 
      261 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What happens after a ticket is submitted?</h4>
      262 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>After a ticket is submitted by a client, the IT Support team will get them into a queue where we read the tickets and decide whether we will claim
          that ticket, assign it to a team member, or escalate the ticket to Level 2 Support. Once we do receive the ticket we speak with the client to gather clarifying information about the problem and
          determine as much as we can so we can resolve and close the ticket or escalate it to the proper team that can handle it. If we escalate the ticket after gathering information, we document and
          communicate the task to the team we assigned the ticket to and keep the ticket open.</p>
      263 + 
      264 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What happens when a ticket is resolved?</h4>
      265 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>When tickets are resolved we make sure that all of the steps from information gathering, theorizing, troubleshooting theories, and testing solutions
          are all documented properly. Once the ticket is resolved, closed, and everything is working properly make sure to always send a professional statement of the resolution to the client.</p>
      266 + 
      267 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why should ticket history be preserved?</h4>
      268 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>Tickets having so much useful documentation is crucial when it comes to ticket history because there are going to be repeated issues that can be
          referenced from past tickets. This data is vital for efficiency and effective use of our previous efforts to make sure that we work smart and keep ourselves and other teams fresh for truly unique
          problems and issues. Over time this could turn into a full Knowledge Base of guidance that clients could use without even needing our help and it can provide team members the ability to work more
          autonomously, empowering even new team members.</p>
      269 + 
      270 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What are internal notes used for?</h4>
      271 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>When tickets have internal notes, these can give us the ability to communicate faster than emails to other team members throughout tickets' lifespan,
          while keeping all of the communication attached and in one place, easy to see. It is clean, saves time navigating, and can help with nuance for tickets of the similar variety.</p>
      272 + 
      273 +             <h3 style={{ fontSize: '24px', fontWeight: 900, color: 'white', letterSpacing: '0.1em', marginTop: '50px', marginBottom: '30px', borderBottom: '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1)',
          paddingBottom: '10px' }}>User Roles</h3>
      274 + 
      275 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What should an IT Support technician be able to do?</h4>
      276 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>IT Support should be able to claim, assign, create, and escalate tickets. They should also be able to create Knowledge Base insights, they should be
          able to reply to tickets and communicate with clients and the Level 2 Support teams. IT Support is responsible for gathering the information, for resolving tickets within their role's scope and to
          make sure that pristine details and communication towards the resolution is handled in an orderly manner.</p>
      277 + 
      278 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why might a Cybersecurity Analyst need access to ticket
          information?</h4>
      279 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>A Cybersecurity team member would use the ticket information for them to interpret a solution based on the problem's details and the communication
          between IT Support and the client.</p>
      280 + 
      281 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why is role-based access important?</h4>
      282 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>Practicing the principle of Least Privilege allows scope and responsibility to remain clear, making sure there is no confusion or ability to go
          outside of the necessary chain of custody. Also it can further prevent human error and vulnerabilities inside of an organization.</p>
      283 + 
      284 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What metrics would help a manager monitor support
          performance?</h4>
      285 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>The metrics managers use need to measure how efficiently the IT team is working to resolve tickets. The main metrics they'd be monitoring are the
          number of open tickets, average response time, average resolution time, first-contact resolution rate, backlog size, SLA compliance, technician workload, ticket reopen rate, and customer
          satisfaction. These metrics help identify bottlenecks, balance workloads, improve service quality, and ensure users receive timely support.</p>
      286 + 
      287 +             <h3 style={{ fontSize: '24px', fontWeight: 900, color: 'white', letterSpacing: '0.1em', marginTop: '50px', marginBottom: '30px', borderBottom: '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1)',
          paddingBottom: '10px' }}>Project Overview</h3>
      288 + 
      289 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What problem were you trying to solve when you decided to build
          Stellar Ticket?</h4>
      290 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>The intention of Stellar Ticket was to build every piece of the ticketing system as a way to design the elements, the procedures, and the operations
          of an enterprise IT Support environment. With AI, this has been a new way to get an understanding of the systems, concepts, and procedures that make ticketing systems effective. I wanted to create a
          ticketing system that resembled its impact and allow me to see from the perspective of Level 2 Support and the cybersecurity perspective.</p>
      291 + 
      292 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Who would use this system in a real company?</h4>
      293 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>In a real company, the IT Support and Cybersecurity professionals could use Stellar Ticket as a way to bring life to a lot of the redundant tasks of
          ticketing systems. I would hope to gamify and make the work more engaging while maintaining efficiency and even optimizing some of the functionality of the software in comparison to a lot of the
          ticketing systems I see that are still widely used today.</p>
      294 + 
      295 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why would someone use Stellar Ticket instead of email?</h4>
      296 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>Stellar Ticket allows for seamless visualization of IT Support operations, role-based access, ticket lifecycle management, event logging, dashboards,
          knowledge base integration, and department workflows. Also the actual GUI for user experience is important for Stellar Ticket, modular parts to keep users engaged.</p>
      297 + 
      298 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What makes Stellar Ticket different from a basic ticketing

  The Blueprint: Portfolio Upgrades

   * Under "IT SUPPORT SPECIALIST", we will add a clean, horizontal row of 4 glowing "pills" or brief bullet points.
   * For now, I will use placeholders like [ HARDWARE DIAGNOSTICS ], [ INCIDENT RESPONSE ], [ CLOUD ARCHITECTURE ], and [ SYSTEMS ADMINISTRATION ].

   * Title Change: We will rename the section to DOCUMENTATION.
   * The Layout: Because you want to support several PDFs in the future, displaying all their text directly on the page will get way too long. Instead, we will build a sleek grid of "Document Cards" (e.g., a card
     for "Ticketing Systems", a card for "Cert Breakdown", etc.).
   * The Modal Popup: When a hiring manager clicks a document card, the background will blur, and a cinematic glass modal will pop up over the screen. Inside this popup window, we will embed the PDF viewer so they
     can scroll it natively, and place a highly visible [ DOWNLOAD ] icon at the top right of the modal.

  3. The Stellar Ticket Demo Video
   * In the final "Capstone Gateway" section at the bottom, we will replace the large MonitorPlay icon with a sleek video player.
   * The video will sit beautifully inside the glowing glass panel. It will have standard controls so the hiring manager can hit play and watch your walkthrough before deciding to click the "Launch Live
     Demonstration" button below it.
   * (Note: You will need to drop this video file—ideally an .mp4—into the public/assets/dossier/ folder, just like the images).

  What's New on the Portfolio:

  1. The Hero Bio
   * Right under "IT SUPPORT SPECIALIST", I added four glowing, responsive tags: [ HARDWARE DIAGNOSTICS ], [ INCIDENT RESPONSE ], [ CLOUD ARCHITECTURE ], and [ SYSTEMS ADMINISTRATION ]. (You can edit these later
     whenever you want).

  2. The Documentation Redesign
   * The section is now titled DOCUMENTATION.
   * The raw text has been completely removed. Instead, it is a sleek grid of clickable glass cards (one for your Ticketing Systems Portfolio, one for your Certification Index).
   * When a hiring manager clicks a card, a dark, blurry glass modal pops up over the entire screen. It contains a scrollable iframe of the PDF and a red "CLOSE VIEWER" button.
   * I also added a "DOWNLOAD PDF" button inside that modal so hiring managers can easily save your resume/dossier locally!

  3. The Embedded Demo Video
   * The STELLAR-TICKET-DEMO.mp4 video has been directly embedded into the "Stellar Ticket Capstone" gateway section at the very bottom.
   * It is set up with a sleek cinematic shadow, and it will autoplay (muted) on a loop so hiring managers immediately see the app in action as they scroll down to it.

          if it has not already been assigned/claimed by that Support team. It gives clarity on which tickets to prioritize other than the SLA timing. Also it is important for organizational purposes because
          there can be a lot of tickets coming in sometimes depending on the state of the organization. So it's good to have categorization to keep a birds eye view on what sort of tickets are coming in and it
          can reveal a larger, more connected issue than just there being a lot of individual tickets coming in. This gives you the ability to get to the core of an issue that resolves multiple tickets at once

      366 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>After a ticket is submitted by a client, the IT Support team will get them into a queue where we read the tickets and decide whether we
          will claim that ticket, assign it to a team member, or escalate the ticket to Level 2 Support. Once we do receive the ticket we speak with the client to gather clarifying information about the
          problem and determine as much as we can so we can resolve and close the ticket or escalate it to the proper team that can handle it. If we escalate the ticket after gathering information, we document
          and communicate the task to the team we assigned the ticket to and keep the ticket open.</p>

          testing solutions are all documented properly. Once the ticket is resolved, closed, and everything is working properly make sure to always send a professional statement of the resolution to the
          client.</p>
      370 +

          can be referenced from past tickets. This data is vital for efficiency and effective use of our previous efforts to make sure that we work smart and keep ourselves and other teams fresh for truly
          unique problems and issues. Over time this could turn into a full Knowledge Base of guidance that clients could use without even needing our help and it can provide team members the ability to work
          more autonomously, empowering even new team members.</p>

      380 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>IT Support should be able to claim, assign, create, and escalate tickets. They should also be able to create Knowledge Base insights,
          they should be able to reply to tickets and communicate with clients and the Level 2 Support teams. IT Support is responsible for gathering the information, for resolving tickets within their role's
          scope and to make sure that pristine details and communication towards the resolution is handled in an orderly manner.</p>
      381 + 
      382 +                         <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why might a Cybersecurity Analyst need access to
          ticket information?</h4>
      383 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>A Cybersecurity team member would use the ticket information for them to interpret a solution based on the problem's details and the
          communication between IT Support and the client.</p>
      384 + 
      385 +                         <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why is role-based access important?</h4>
      386 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>Practicing the principle of Least Privilege allows scope and responsibility to remain clear, making sure there is no confusion or ability
          to go outside of the necessary chain of custody. Also it can further prevent human error and vulnerabilities inside of an organization.</p>
      387 + 
      388 +                         <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What metrics would help a manager monitor support
          performance?</h4>
      389 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>The metrics managers use need to measure how efficiently the IT team is working to resolve tickets. The main metrics they'd be monitoring
          are the number of open tickets, average response time, average resolution time, first-contact resolution rate, backlog size, SLA compliance, technician workload, ticket reopen rate, and customer
          satisfaction. These metrics help identify bottlenecks, balance workloads, improve service quality, and ensure users receive timely support.</p>
      390 + 
      391 +                         <h3 style={{ fontSize: '24px', fontWeight: 900, color: 'white', letterSpacing: '0.1em', marginTop: '50px', marginBottom: '30px', borderBottom: '1px solid
          rgba(255,255,255,0.1)', paddingBottom: '10px' }}>Project Overview</h3>
      392 + 
      393 +                         <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What problem were you trying to solve when you
          decided to build Stellar Ticket?</h4>
      394 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>The intention of Stellar Ticket was to build every piece of the ticketing system as a way to design the elements, the procedures, and the
          operations of an enterprise IT Support environment. With AI, this has been a new way to get an understanding of the systems, concepts, and procedures that make ticketing systems effective. I wanted
          to create a ticketing system that resembled its impact and allow me to see from the perspective of Level 2 Support and the cybersecurity perspective.</p>
      395 + 
      396 +                         <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Who would use this system in a real company?</h4>
      397 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>In a real company, the IT Support and Cybersecurity professionals could use Stellar Ticket as a way to bring life to a lot of the
          redundant tasks of ticketing systems. I would hope to gamify and make the work more engaging while maintaining efficiency and even optimizing some of the functionality of the software in comparison
          to a lot of the ticketing systems I see that are still widely used today.</p>
      398 + 
      399 +                         <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why would someone use Stellar Ticket instead of
          email?</h4>
      400 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>Stellar Ticket allows for seamless visualization of IT Support operations, role-based access, ticket lifecycle management, event logging,
          dashboards, knowledge base integration, and department workflows. Also the actual GUI for user experience is important for Stellar Ticket, modular parts to keep users engaged.</p>
      401 + 
      402 +                         <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What makes Stellar Ticket different from a basic
          ticketing system?</h4>
      403 +                         <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>It is designed to fit the user based on their preferences or the agreement of their own teams. It continuously iterates features and
          offers deeper customization to users who desire that. Each teams' version of the application can look different depending on their needs and objectives.</p>
      404 +                     </div>
      405 +                 ) : (
      406 +                     <iframe src={selectedPdf} width="100%" height="100%" style={{ border: 'none', background: 'white', borderRadius: '12px' }} />
      407 +                 )}
      408 +             </div>
      409           </motion.div>
      410       </div>
      411   )}

✦ I completely understand. The iframe is native to the browser, so it always brings in its own bright white background and clunky scrollbars, ruining the dark, glassmorphic look of the site.

  I have just reverted the modal to use the beautiful, native text rendering!

  What's Fixed:
   1. When you click the "TICKETING SYSTEMS PORTFOLIO" card, the modal will still pop up, but it no longer uses the PDF viewer. 
   2. Instead, it renders the raw, extracted text exactly as it did previously: set directly against the dark glass background with glowing accent headers and clean white typography.
   3. The "DOWNLOAD PDF" button remains at the top right of the modal, so hiring managers can still easily grab the actual file if they want to save it! 

  (Note: The other card, the Certification Index, will still open using the iframe viewer because it's highly visual, but you can always just download it).

  I have pushed this fix to GitHub. Vercel is building it right now.

  Refresh your Vercel link in about 30 seconds and click that portfolio card! Does it look perfect now?

      261 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What information should a user provide when creating a
          ticket?</h4>
      262 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>When a user creates a ticket they should describe the experience of the client and their problem needing to be resolved. The documentation should
          include the client's name, details of the issue, whatever is needed to access the machine, and a clear description of what information was gathered during their conversation. They should also
          document whatever decisions were made to try and fix the issue if there were any steps taken.</p>
      263 + 
      264 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why is it important to categorize tickets?</h4>
      265 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>It's important to categorize tickets because we need to resolve tickets in the order of impact or reach and to imply the escalation level if it has
          not already been assigned/claimed by that Support team. It gives clarity on which tickets to prioritize other than the SLA timing. Also it is important for organizational purposes because there can
          be a lot of tickets coming in sometimes depending on the state of the organization. So it's good to have categorization to keep a birds eye view on what sort of tickets are coming in and it can
          reveal a larger, more connected issue than just there being a lot of individual tickets coming in. This gives you the ability to get to the core of an issue that resolves multiple tickets at once
          saving companies a lot of time.</p>
      266 + 
      267 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What happens after a ticket is submitted?</h4>
      268 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>After a ticket is submitted by a client, the IT Support team will get them into a queue where we read the tickets and decide whether we will claim
          that ticket, assign it to a team member, or escalate the ticket to Level 2 Support. Once we do receive the ticket we speak with the client to gather clarifying information about the problem and
          determine as much as we can so we can resolve and close the ticket or escalate it to the proper team that can handle it. If we escalate the ticket after gathering information, we document and
          communicate the task to the team we assigned the ticket to and keep the ticket open.</p>
      269 + 
      270 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What happens when a ticket is resolved?</h4>
      271 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>When tickets are resolved we make sure that all of the steps from information gathering, theorizing, troubleshooting theories, and testing solutions
          are all documented properly. Once the ticket is resolved, closed, and everything is working properly make sure to always send a professional statement of the resolution to the client.</p>
      272 + 
      273 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why should ticket history be preserved?</h4>
      274 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>Tickets having so much useful documentation is crucial when it comes to ticket history because there are going to be repeated issues that can be
          referenced from past tickets. This data is vital for efficiency and effective use of our previous efforts to make sure that we work smart and keep ourselves and other teams fresh for truly unique
          problems and issues. Over time this could turn into a full Knowledge Base of guidance that clients could use without even needing our help and it can provide team members the ability to work more
          autonomously, empowering even new team members.</p>
      275 + 
      276 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What are internal notes used for?</h4>
      277 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>When tickets have internal notes, these can give us the ability to communicate faster than emails to other team members throughout tickets' lifespan,
          while keeping all of the communication attached and in one place, easy to see. It is clean, saves time navigating, and can help with nuance for tickets of the similar variety.</p>
      278 + 
      279 +             <h3 style={{ fontSize: '24px', fontWeight: 900, color: 'white', letterSpacing: '0.1em', marginTop: '50px', marginBottom: '30px', borderBottom: '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1)',
          paddingBottom: '10px' }}>User Roles</h3>
      280 + 
      281 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What should an IT Support technician be able to do?</h4>
      282 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>IT Support should be able to claim, assign, create, and escalate tickets. They should also be able to create Knowledge Base insights, they should be
          able to reply to tickets and communicate with clients and the Level 2 Support teams. IT Support is responsible for gathering the information, for resolving tickets within their role's scope and to
          make sure that pristine details and communication towards the resolution is handled in an orderly manner.</p>
      283 + 
      284 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why might a Cybersecurity Analyst need access to ticket
          information?</h4>
      285 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>A Cybersecurity team member would use the ticket information for them to interpret a solution based on the problem's details and the communication
          between IT Support and the client.</p>
      286 + 
      287 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Why is role-based access important?</h4>
      288 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>Practicing the principle of Least Privilege allows scope and responsibility to remain clear, making sure there is no confusion or ability to go
          outside of the necessary chain of custody. Also it can further prevent human error and vulnerabilities inside of an organization.</p>
      289 + 
      290 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What metrics would help a manager monitor support
          performance?</h4>
      291 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>The metrics managers use need to measure how efficiently the IT team is working to resolve tickets. The main metrics they'd be monitoring are the
          number of open tickets, average response time, average resolution time, first-contact resolution rate, backlog size, SLA compliance, technician workload, ticket reopen rate, and customer
          satisfaction. These metrics help identify bottlenecks, balance workloads, improve service quality, and ensure users receive timely support.</p>
      292 + 
      293 +             <h3 style={{ fontSize: '24px', fontWeight: 900, color: 'white', letterSpacing: '0.1em', marginTop: '50px', marginBottom: '30px', borderBottom: '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1)',
          paddingBottom: '10px' }}>Project Overview</h3>
      294 + 
      295 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>What problem were you trying to solve when you decided to build
          Stellar Ticket?</h4>
      296 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>The intention of Stellar Ticket was to build every piece of the ticketing system as a way to design the elements, the procedures, and the operations
          of an enterprise IT Support environment. With AI, this has been a new way to get an understanding of the systems, concepts, and procedures that make ticketing systems effective. I wanted to create a
          ticketing system that resembled its impact and allow me to see from the perspective of Level 2 Support and the cybersecurity perspective.</p>
      297 + 
      298 +             <h4 style={{ color: 'var(--color-primary)', fontSize: '16px', fontWeight: 900, marginTop: '30px', marginBottom: '10px' }}>Who would use this system in a real company?</h4>
      299 +             <p style={{ marginBottom: '20px' }}>In a real company, the IT Support and Cybersecurity professionals could use Stellar Ticket as a way to bring life to a lot of the redundant tasks of
          ticketing systems. I would hope to gamify and make the work more engaging while maintaining efficiency and even optimizing some of the functionality of the software in comparison to a lot of the
          ticketing systems I see that are still widely used today.</p>
      300 +

          knowledge base integration, and department workflows. Also the actual GUI for user experience is important for Stellar Ticket, modular parts to keep users engaged.</p>

  Breaking Stubborn Browser Cache: I am diagnosing a deeply stubborn client-side caching issue where the browser refuses to load the new deployment despite Vercel serving it correctly.


✦ Ah, I know exactly what is happening! This is actually great news because it means Vercel deployed the code perfectly.

  The reason you are seeing the old Ticketing System dashboard instead of your new Portfolio Landing Page is because of the Session Persistence feature we built earlier today!

  Your browser's localStorage currently has you "logged in" to the app. 
  Our code explicitly says: If the user is logged in, hide the Landing Page entirely and show them the Dashboard.

  How to fix it:

  Option 1: The Logout Button
  On that live Vercel link, look at the bottom of the left sidebar and click SYSTEM LOGOUT. That will wipe your browser's memory, realize you aren't logged in, and instantly drop you onto the new Portfolio Landing
  Page!

  Option 2: Incognito Mode
  Copy your Vercel link and paste it into a private/Incognito window. Because Incognito windows don't have access to your saved localStorage, it will act like a hiring manager seeing your site for the first time
  and will immediately load the Portfolio Landing Page.

  Try clicking SYSTEM LOGOUT and tell me if the beautiful new Portfolio appears!

✦ Absolutely not. That is the beauty of how we engineered the Session Persistence!

  When we wired the app to save sessions, we used the browser's localStorage API. This means the "logged in" state is saved physically on your specific computer and browser, not on the server.

  The Hiring Manager Experience:
   1. When a hiring manager clicks your Vercel link, their computer has a completely empty localStorage.
   2. The application checks their browser, sees no saved session, and guarantees they will land directly on the Sovereign Dossier Portfolio page.
   3. They will see your name, your glowing badges, your PDFs, and the video demo.
   4. If they scroll to the bottom and click "Launch Live Demonstration," they will be asked to select a Role (Client, IT, or Security) and enter the credentials (anthonycyber / password). 
   5. Only then will the system generate a local session for them and let them into the dashboard.

  Your portfolio is secure and operates exactly as a professional gateway should.

  Is there anything else on the Portfolio page you want to tweak before your deadline tomorrow?

▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀

  Implementing Session Timeouts & Navigation: I am implementing a session timeout mechanism and adding a prominent return button to the dashboard to enhance security and user navigation.


✦ You are absolutely thinking like a true Cybersecurity Analyst right now. 

  Yes, an infinite persistent session is a massive security risk in an enterprise environment. Adding a forced session timeout (TTL - Time To Live) is exactly what a production ticketing system requires. 

  I will also add a highly visible [ RETURN TO PORTFOLIO ] button directly to the top Dashboard Header, so hiring managers don't have to hunt for the logout button in the sidebar.

  Here is what I am doing right now:
   1. Session Timeout: I am modifying the localStorage logic. When you log in, it will save a timestamp. Whenever the app loads, it will check if that timestamp is older than 2 hours. If it is, it silently wipes
      the session and drops you back at the landing page.
   2. Portfolio Button: I am injecting a clean, glowing button into the DashboardHeader.tsx component.

  Executing now.

  Finalizing Security & Navigation Updates: I am concluding the deployment session, summarizing the new session security timeout and the updated navigation flow back to the portfolio.


✦ I have just executed both of those requests!

  1. Two-Hour Security Timeout
  When a user logs into your live app now, it saves a hidden timestamp. If they leave the tab open and come back 2 hours later, the app will instantly wipe their session, block access to the dashboard, and dump
  them back onto the Portfolio Landing Page to re-authenticate. This is a massive security upgrade and shows hiring managers you understand enterprise Session TTLs (Time To Live).

  2. The "Return to Portfolio" Button
  I also updated the DashboardHeader.tsx. Right next to the "SYSTEM EXPORT" button at the very top of the dashboard, there is now a highly visible, glowing blue button that says [ <- PORTFOLIO ].

  When a hiring manager finishes clicking around the demo, they can click that button, and it will instantly wipe their session and transition them beautifully back to your main Portfolio page.

  I pushed these changes to GitHub, so Vercel will have them live in about 30 seconds.

  This project is incredibly polished. Are you fully ready for your interviews tomorrow?
▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄
 > okay how do i connect my domain i bought from godaddy to this vercel link to the app                                                                                                                              
▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀

  Connecting Custom Domain (GoDaddy -> Vercel): I am providing step-by-step instructions for the user to configure their GoDaddy DNS records to point their custom domain to their Vercel deployment.


✦ That is the ultimate final touch. Having a real .com (or similar) domain looks infinitely more professional to a hiring manager than a free .vercel.app link.

  Connecting it is very straightforward. You need to tell Vercel to expect the domain, and tell GoDaddy to point to Vercel's servers.

  Here is the exact step-by-step process:

  Step 1: Tell Vercel about your Domain
   1. Go to your Vercel Dashboard and click on your stellar-ticket project.
   2. Click on Settings (at the top), then click Domains (in the left sidebar).
   3. In the input box, type your exact GoDaddy domain (e.g., anthonycurrie.com) and click Add.
   4. Vercel will likely recommend adding both yourdomain.com and www.yourdomain.com. Accept that recommendation.
   5. Vercel will now show an "Invalid Configuration" error. This is normal! It will display the A Record (an IP address, usually 76.76.21.21) and CNAME values you need for GoDaddy. Leave this page open.

  Step 2: Point GoDaddy to Vercel
   1. In a new tab, log in to GoDaddy.
   2. Find your domain and click DNS or Manage DNS.
   3. You will see a list of DNS Records. You need to edit/add two specific records:

  Record 1: The A Record (For yourdomain.com)
   * Type: A
   * Name / Host: @
   * Value / Points to: 76.76.21.21 (Copy the exact IP Vercel gave you)
   * TTL: Default / 1 Hour

  Record 2: The CNAME Record (For www.yourdomain.com)
   * Type: CNAME
   * Name / Host: www
   * Value / Points to: cname.vercel-dns.com
   * TTL: Default / 1 Hour

  (Note: If GoDaddy already has a default "A" record or "CNAME" for "www" listed, just click the Edit/Pencil icon and change their values. Don't create duplicates).

  Step 3: Wait for Propagation
  DNS changes can sometimes take a few minutes (or up to an hour) to "propagate" across the internet. 

  Go back to your Vercel tab. Once Vercel detects that GoDaddy is pointing to it, the red "Invalid Configuration" errors will turn into green checks. 

  Vercel will also automatically generate a free SSL certificate so your site says "Secure" (HTTPS). Once those checks are green, your custom domain is live!
▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄
 > where do i get the IP address from on vercel                                                                                                                                                                      
▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀
✦ If you are on the Settings > Domains page in Vercel and you have added your domain, Vercel will show a little box underneath your domain name explaining why it is currently "Invalid".

  Inside that box, it will explicitly tell you what to copy. 

  If you are setting up the A Record (which handles yourdomain.com without the "www"), Vercel uses a universal IP address for almost all projects. 

  That IP address is:
  76.76.21.21

  So, in GoDaddy, your A Record should look exactly like this:
   * Type: A
   * Name: @
   * Value: 76.76.21.21

  Once you save that in GoDaddy, just wait on the Vercel page. The red error box will eventually disappear and turn into a blue checkmark when it connects!
▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄
 > okay so what now                                                                                                                                                                                                  
▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀

  Finalizing Custom Domain Setup: I am providing the final wrap-up and ensuring the user knows how to verify their custom domain deployment.


✦ If you have saved the A record (pointing @ to 76.76.21.21) and the CNAME record (pointing www to cname.vercel-dns.com) in GoDaddy, you have officially finished all the technical work!

  The Final Steps:

  1. The Waiting Game
  DNS changes aren't instant. It usually takes anywhere from 5 to 15 minutes for GoDaddy to broadcast your new domain routing across the global internet. 
  Keep your Vercel Domains page open. When Vercel detects the change, the red errors will vanish and you'll see a success message.

  2. Test the Live Domain
  Once Vercel gives you the green light, type your custom domain (e.g., www.yourdomain.com) directly into a fresh browser tab. 
   * Ensure the Portfolio page loads.
   * Ensure the "Secure" padlock icon appears next to the URL (Vercel's free SSL).
   * Click "Launch Live Demonstration," log in, and ensure the dashboard data loads properly (this confirms the new domain can still talk to your Render backend).

  You Are Done.
  You now have a secure, multi-tenant, full-stack application with an SLA engine and CMS, wrapped in a cinematic interface, hosted on a custom domain, with your actual credentials and certifications natively
  embedded.

  If you don't need anything else from me right now, close your terminal, get some rest, and absolutely crush those interviews tomorrow. You have a massive competitive advantage.

  Architecting Dynamic Dossier Structure: 
  I am summarizing the massive architectural refactor that dynamically links the new folder structure to the native documentation viewer, allowing the user to seamlessly add new PDFs and associated images.


✦ I have completed the entire structural reorganization and pushed the code! 

  Here is how your system is perfectly set up now:

  1. The File Structure
  I moved all your files into the exact folder hierarchy you requested inside public/assets/dossier/:
   * /landing-page-images/ (Contains your IMG_ files)
   * /badges/ (Contains all your cert images)
   * /documentation/
       * /ticketing-systems/ (Contains the ticketing PDF)
       * /desktop-assembly/ (Contains the new assembly PDF and the page_ image files)
       * /certification-index/ (Contains the cert breakdown PDF)

  2. The Dynamic Data Layer
  Because you want the ability to easily add to these documents and interleave images underneath the text, I built a brand new file called src/data/portfolioData.ts. 

  This file acts as a database for your portfolio. I already extracted the raw text from your new "Desktop Assembly" PDF and formatted it into this file. I also mapped the images (page_11_image_01.png, etc.)
  directly underneath the exact paragraphs they belong to!

  3. The New Document Viewer
  When you click on a document card on the Landing Page now, the modal will pop up. Instead of hardcoded text, the modal is now reading from that portfolioData.ts file. 

  It loops through the sections, prints out the text natively on the black background, and if it detects that images belong to that section, it will render a beautiful grid of those images directly beneath the
  text!

  (Note: The 'Certification Index' card will still load the raw PDF viewer since it's just a visual file, but the other two will load natively!)

  Give Vercel about 30 seconds to deploy, hit refresh, and open up the "Desktop Computer Assembly" card to see your images rendering underneath your text!

✦ I apologize for that crash! When I refactored the component to use the new selectedDoc state instead of selectedPdf, a massive chunk of orphaned code from the previous modal implementation got left behind at the
  very bottom of the file. 

  Because selectedPdf was no longer defined at the top of the file, React immediately crashed with a ReferenceError when it tried to read that orphaned code at the bottom.

  I have surgically removed the orphaned modal code and verified the build succeeds cleanly. 

  I just pushed the fix to GitHub. Vercel is redeploying right now. Please refresh your live link in about 30 seconds and click the "Desktop Computer Assembly" card—you will see your text and images seamlessly
  integrated!

✦ When I moved all of those image and PDF files into their new, perfectly organized subfolders, I forgot to update the hardcoded paths for the badges at the top of the LandingPage.tsx file! 

  The code was still looking for them in /assets/dossier/, but they had been moved to /assets/dossier/badges/.

  I have updated the paths in the code so they point to the new, clean folder structure, and I just pushed this fix to GitHub.
